62378720
>>62378686
you just launched a whole interdimensional TED Talk powered by three screenshots, two podcasts, and a gas-station energy drink.
You're standing on a folding chair yelling, "EVERY SINGLE WOMAN CAN JUST BECOME RICH, DATE A CELEBRITY, FIND A SUGAR DADDY, OR MARRY SOME DEVOTED GUY," like life is a character-select screen in a video game where everybody spawns with max charisma and infinite social options.
My brother in chaos, that's not how reality works.
You took a handful of visible examples, put them in a blender, added internet rage, and came out with a theory that somehow explains half the human population. That's clown astronomy. You're looking at three bright stars and declaring you've mapped the entire galaxy.
And then when someone tells a story about a rude interaction on a dating app, your brain teleports straight back to the same speech. Like the conversation took a bus stop and you arrived riding a shopping cart with no wheels.
The wild part is that your argument assumes every person has equal opportunities, equal looks, equal luck, equal social skills, equal location, equal connections, and equal willingness to do the things you're listing. That's a level of simplification so aggressive it could flatten a mountain into a parking lot.
Reality is messy. Some people struggle. Some people succeed. Some get lucky. Some don't. Some are attractive and still miserable. Some are average and find great relationships. Human lives aren't assembled from four preset buttons.
So when you scream "EVERY SINGLE ONE," that's the clown horn giving itself CPR.
The issue isn't that you're making a point. The issue is that you're treating a giant, complicated reality like a meme template and acting surprised when people look confused.
You're not describing the world.
You're describing a cartoon version of the world that lives rent-free inside your own echo chamber.

Seems like an important thing to know.

62382877
>>62381049
27m, 150k, fully remote. on track to become senior and make ~180k

swe

62382899
>>62381049
24m
$45/hr as a Information Management/Technical Consultant.
Been at it for 4 years:
-Mostly just showing people how to organize data and operate basic proprietary corpo programs.
-90% of the work is physical (walking in, seeing something has obviously been done wrong, fixing it)
Job security is that people will always have trouble with the machines they use on a day to day basis. People will always be stupid and mess things up.

>>62382184
On the equipment side, stenotype's have been around since the 1910's so you can easily get comfortable with the layout on a typewriter from the 1950's for $30.

>>62382303
>Weekly hours?
It's rural, my usual is roughly 30 hours. I get calls from 100+ miles away who are willing to pay enough to have me come out and teach.
A lot of the extra dough comes from just claiming what the company wants to through out tech wise, then wiping and reselling their entire server (made in 2021) online or in person through third party marketplaces. Make about double what the hourly is with that.

>>62382530
>how did you get started
I joke to a lot of the upper class clients "no one hired me, so I hired myself"

>>62382877
Is it niche work? How do you prevent your job from getting jeeted out?

62382947
>>62382899
>Is it niche work? How do you prevent your job from getting jeeted out?

we have some foreign contractors but we have a shit ton of funding covering burn so we can afford to hire citizens. it's not a unique business but we're niche in our approach and field (data/ai). we're actually hiring right now.

62383011
33m 110k a year working in crypto, remote.
Workplace changed from based and nice to kinda idiotic thanks to poor business decisions, AI brain rot, and limited runway.

I'm trying to change but everything else pays like shit or is a cult. IT makes me want to kill myself at this point. I have golden handcuffs

I just want to find a way to accumulate so much capital that I can live off dividends.

62383016
32k CAD ($20.25/hr) after being at WMT for 10 years
I know it's not the best job, but it's low effort, my coworkers and bosses and customers love me, and I can keep working sustainably. I've managed to get about 140k CAD net worth, too.

62383085
>>62382530
Basically I got hired from a craigslist posting for a middleman tech company ran by some bulgarian. He would send us out to clients and pay us less than half, scalping the rest. I have a legitimate interest in tech and build things with hardware, networks and software so some of these places I helped set up and was friendly helpful to them. It also let me learn the missing parts of each field and then I was able to piece it all together. Since they were the clients, I would interact with the CEOs, Owners and top end leadership of each company. You vibe well with them and not be inappropriate (hitting on, trying to get numbers, be a creep) they will want you, even do power plays to get you. I quit the middleman cause he would send me to do shit that gets paid 170k salary (85/hr) and pay me 21/hr and scalp the other 64, while not helping me really. I was able to chill for a year, then i reached out to one of these clients and they picked me right up. Since they wanted me, I had the leverage to negotiate a higher salary, which they had no problem paying. They liked me so much they just have me run the whole IT department, I dont even really have oversight. I do have to know my shit, but AI makes it 1000x easier and if you arent using AI you arent gonna make it. Literally complete tasks that would take months to do in days. These guys can't even fathom that kind of efficiency so they think im busy all the time. AI is literally a cheat code, especially if you know your shit and use it to do that shit. I'm close to 6 figs starting, will easily hit 6 figs after solve some of their money loss headaches (which i have done for a major part).
>Getting interest from potential employers gives you huge leverage, esp if they know you are a game changer
The best part is I asked if I could start my own projects for learning, that they would benefit the company and the CEO is hyped for it. I am literally getting paid to self improve

62383096

item.title: My x feed has turn into this constantly. — 24 replies — 44.8/hr
item.content:encoded:
My x feed has turn into this constantly.

Open thread • Replies: 24 • Updated: 2026-06-18 20:33 UTC

OP

My x feed has turn into this constantly.

1) some "omfg" Claude workflow that will make you 60k/month
2) retards selling courses/coaching/mentorships in how to run a gazillion automated YT/tiktok/whatever pages with screenshots of students making money
3) various clickbait: "MrBeast shares key insights", "This guy just shared to how to make 10k a month with sleep channels", "this guy just made $2,582,285 on a memecoin"

It never ends. And now with AI the effort on having to actually write something catchy isn't there. It's just constant slop with supposed cheat codes to create slop that you can monetize.

The reality is this:
-The slop on the image makes no sense, you cannot replace a Bloomberg terminal with AI because a Bloomberg terminal gets emerging constant new sources that you don't have. Of course im not saying paying for a Bloomberg terminal is going to give you an edge and make you money. But just proving my point. Also all of these "free" workflows always have a paywall somewhere.
-Course selling scammers make money from the courses, this is not news.
-Clickbaiters make money from the traffic.

No one is making money doing something new and cool. By no one I mean, 99% of people. Everything is slop. Anyone making money is doing it from some sort of loophole. It's literally everywhere. There is always something dodgy going on.

For everything cool and genuine (like John Carmack inveting Doom in 1993 and getting rich because the game was just that good and revolutionary) 99% of people that get rich are doing it scaling slop. It will only get worse as AI gets trained on AI generated slop.

And if you try to profit from this, it's just algorithm luck to get your account ranked by the system so your posts get anywhere. Everything is literally a lottery, unless your genius is so good that it eventually thrives even if you are viewthrottled by the algorithm, but that is insanely rare.

62382855
>>62382838

Let's talk math. Given that the US must roll over a third of its near-zero interest debt to market rates this year, and given current trends in Federal spending and revenue, and given current interest rates, the interest on the debt ALONE will exceed all Federal revenue in about ten years. And that is a conservative estimate because it does not take into account waning interest in US treasury paper, the loss of revenue from the current recession, and the fact that interest rates will almost certainly begin climbing rapidly as the deadline approaches. So what then? There is no political will to make the drastic cuts in spending that would be needed to turn the ship around. When the USA starts paying the mortgage with a credit card, the MMT scam is finished. Not the end of the world, but the end of the American empire. That is for sure.

item.title: Questions from a Mid-20’s Investor — 52 replies — 11.6/hr
item.content:encoded:
Questions from a Mid-20’s Investor

Open thread • Replies: 52 • Updated: 2026-06-18 16:37 UTC

OP

Hello /biz/ I will try and keep this brief
Currently I have been investing most of my take home pay simply into VOO as I only just recently started investing
It seemed simple, solid mid-long term option, and sensical for starting out to not fuck up while getting exposure
Now, this has nothing to do with FOMO, but I’m wondering if I should start aiming towards investing in either specific sector ETFs or maybe specific stocks to chase higher returns
My portfolio is currently at $60K mostly in VOO, perhaps with enough DCA I can reach $100k by years end or at least close to that, depends on growth
Currently my total portfolio return ranges from 17-20% depending on the month for context
I’ve seen recently how well things like SOXX have been doing and was considering finding a sector ETF or two to go into since I already have so much broad market exposure
To be clear, this is not me chasing returns based off past performance, I am well aware growth has to come from somewhere and is not infinite so I’m considering CAGRs as far back as possible
With all this in mind, any recommendations? Find a good sector ETF, go into a specific stock, or maybe just focus on getting more income first and then continue to just VOO and chill
And no I don’t wanna sell options doing covered calls or buy your shit coin etc etc
I should add the sectors I’m interested in are semi-conductors, energy (ones that deal with technology needed to cool data centers), and perhaps drones / nuclear for commercial and military usage

Early replies (first 3)

62369867
>>62369835
XLK
SOXL

62369880
>>62369835
Recreate the ETF so you avoid paying management fees.. Not super relevant for VOO, but if you go into niche sector specific ETFs, the management fees are usually over 0.5%

But otherwise you’re just advising do some tech and semiconductor either way

>It seems like this AI shit isn’t going away any time
It's not, this shit is going to fundamentally change most industries in a way that most people cannot even understand. Its primary limitation is just that older generations can't adapt to it, which means it has naysayers now and a clear path of progressive growth over time, similar to computing decades ago.

It's not just AI though honestly. These companies are the future. With population growth leveling out worldwide, there's not going to be a sudden expansion of demand for food, for minerals, for many traditional industries. NASDAQ is better primed for investment in future tech, hence why I prefer it. Or tech ETFs like VGT, but those are less diversified. Just sinking 50/50 into SP500 and NASDAQ might be fine for you to let it ride

item.title: I made a stupidly autistic workbook to track and project all of my income and expenses and account balances. — 19 replies — 11.4/hr
item.content:encoded:
I made a stupidly autistic workbook to track and project all of my income and expenses and account balances.

Open thread • Replies: 19 • Updated: 2026-06-18 19:25 UTC

OP

I made a stupidly autistic workbook to track and project all of my income and expenses and account balances. i have nobody else to show it to. enjoy.

the payroll calculation sheet includes the full IRS steps that my company's payroll system uses to calculate withholding and everything so i can predict nearly to the penny what my take-home pay will be given a certain number of hours worked, etc

the 401k and home equity numbers don't take growth into account, they're just starting with a recent balance and adding principal deposits only

this has had the neat effect of showing me exactly how grievous an error it is not to max out my 401k contribution up to my employer's match, because i can directly see how much net cash i'm leaving on the table (especially summed at year-end) by doing so, even if i have less liquid cash

Early replies (first 3)

62384424
So i actually like it. Shame you dont have a blank template to share with everyone to try out.

62384465
>>62384414
for predicting utility bills, i have logged all of my utility bills since we got this house in 2022 so i can predict with decent accuracy how much my energy or water bill will be for a decent month

i took all of my planned, regular transactions across all of my accounts, including all of the quarterly and yearly bills like annual insurance premiums and kids' summer camp registrations and blah blah blah and created repeating transaction series just extending outward into future dates.

so for utilities, i have planned transactions for each month that take the monthly average energy/water and then add like 30% margin and I'm always covered and usually have some gravy left over.

for day-to-day household spending, i just transfer my wife 1700 for all groceries and goods for the month (family of 5 + dog, that number is for all household goods including paper towels, detergent, baby diapers, dish soap, all that shit adds up so fast), and then put a lump sum in our joint account for 'family discretionary' expenses and gas for the cars. family discretionary is, obviously, discretionary shit like haircuts, going to the zoo, clothes for us or the kids, gifts for other people, the rare chick-fil-a trip. i don't track/project the nitty gritty there, it's just a lump allocation every month.

62384502
>>62384424
it'd take me a bit to strip my shit out, may do so if the thread is still up later. there's nothing groundbreaking here though, it's just very personalized to my favorite excel methods (tables and structured references and so forth).

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62384615
i can predict exactly how much nitpicking this screenshot will invite, but doing THIS was a colossal aid in planning, all of this shit that's non-monthly or on weird intervals and is generally hard to properly plan for. I laid it all out and then sum up the annual total, split that into a per-month set-aside (rounded up) that i auto-transfer into a separate checking account, and now this stuff never comes as a surprise.

making the transaction log was the natural consequence, where I projected these out onto future dates and plotted what the account balance will be, so i can see if i need to shore it up so it won't go negative in six months or whatever.

62384640
Thats a lot of work. Ive been able to predict my balances 4 years out to a high degree of accuracy just doing an exponential on my saving rate

62384675
>>62384414
too autistic simplify it or you'll get split focus

62384681
>>62384640
i consider myself very bad with money. and having kids has thrown a lot of disruption into the mix. i have had nearly no success with cash savings for the past few years, just spinning my wheels. as i've earned more money you can see a lot of lifestyle creep and bloat in those set-aside numbers for sure.

62384691
>>62384675
the actual interaction is, put my transactions in the list, update my predicted pay hours and then my actual payroll numbers if necessary. that's about it. this isn't for day-to-day spending, that'd be too much work to track like this.

62385134
>>62385071
systems engineer, so both. On spacecraft programs I run the massive systems analysis budgets that account for the mass and power consumption of every single component on the spacecraft, and a bunch of other shit.

major use is monitoring how mass and power growth are trending as we progress through the development cycle, accounting for known-unknowns and unknown-unknowns, to make sure we don't exceed our launch vehicle capability and to make sure for the planned mission orbit the power consumption won't cause the battery to dip too low during eclipse. When the vehicle has been modeled in CAD, I pull the mass properties and predict center of mass evolution as propellant is used up and the spacecraft is in different mechanical configurations (i.e. with solar arrays and instrumentation booms deployed or stowed)

i vibe coded a cute database service with a little node.js web interface for storing the equipment data (and more importantly letting other engineers update that data with good change tracking so we can keep track of who changed what when and why), and then i have excel using power query to fetch all that data and do all the analysis. Using power query is my secret sauce and it regularly blows peoples minds when i show them this secret shit going on in the background.
